codex.title: The Wall of the Faithless
codex.body: In the City of Judgement, souls who served no god are mortared alive into a vast wall and left to dissolve into nothing. Not punished. Erased. The Realms' quietest cruelty â and the wound at the heart of this saga.

codex.title: The Returned
codex.body: You died in the harvest beneath Baldur's Gate, and something pulled you back. The dead do not come back. You did. The crack that let you through is a crack in time itself.

codex.title: The Art of the Duel (combat actions)
codex.body:
Each turn you have a move and one action. Beyond attacking and casting, your action can buy:
â¢ DEFEND (G) â attacks against you have disadvantage until your next turn.
â¢ DASH (F) â extra movement equal to your speed this turn.
â¢ HELP (T) â aid an adjacent ally; their next attack has advantage.
â¢ DISENGAGE (X) â your movement this turn provokes no opportunity attacks.
â¢ SHOVE (V) â a Strength contest to push an adjacent enemy back a tile.
â¢ QUAFF (Q) â drink a healing draught from the party stash (the HUD shows how many remain).
Three rules reward position: leaving an enemy's reach without Disengaging draws an OPPORTUNITY ATTACK; FLANKING a foe (an ally directly opposite it) gives advantage in melee; and firing a ranged attack through an interposing body grants the target half COVER (+2 AC). A foe dropped below half health flashes BLOODIED. Casters' leveled spells cost a slot (shown as L1âL5 on the button) â spend them wisely.

codex.title: Baldur's Gate
codex.body: A great mercantile city on the Sword Coast, ruled by coin and the Flaming Fist, stacked steep from the harbor's Outer City slums to the Patriar estates above. You died in its lower wards and were Returned there. The Gate does not love its poor, but its poor keep their own quiet ledgers of who treated them like they counted â and yours, it turns out, is one of the names they keep.

codex.title: The Returned, Becoming
codex.body: You started as a question the universe asked by accident â a soul that wouldn't dissolve, thrown back through a crack. Somewhere between the Cinderhaunt and the Breach you stopped being an accident and started being a *choice*: every grave you honored, every companion you let matter, every cruelty you refused to inherit. The dead come back blank. You are filling yourself in, deed by deed, with the one thing the Wall can't erase â a person other people would refuse to forget.

codex.title: Aldric Morn â the First Mask
codex.body: Once the Sword Coast's finest Doomguide of Kelemvor. He watched his godless daughter mortared into the Wall â and broke. Now he hunts the Crown of Horns to depose the god of the dead and tear the Wall down forever, no matter who among the living he must spend.

codex.title: The Crown of Horns
codex.body: Forged in the Time of Troubles from the skull of Myrkul, the dead god of death. To wear it is to inherit his hunger. The Crown does not grant power; it spends the wearer to feed itself.

codex.title: The Unmade â the Second Mask
codex.body: The accreted nothing of every soul the cosmos ever threw away â all the Faithless who dissolved, gathered into a single appetite that wants only to finish the erasing. It is not evil. It is the logical end of a wall built to forget people.

codex.title: The Last Returned â the Third Mask
codex.body: A hunter who wears your own face: the you from a future that already lost, walking back through history to stop yourself from making its mistakes. It does not die when struck â it kneels, when its Echoes fall, because it remembers being you.

codex.title: The Lady in the Margins â the Fourth Mask
codex.body: A fifth-dimensional reader who has been with you the whole way â the storyteller at the fire, the voice in the Vault, the one who keeps 'monitoring the situation.' She is what Naeve becomes, ascended out of the story and back into its margins. She does not want to win. She wants to see how it ends â and whether you can surprise her.

codex.title: Sister Garrow
codex.body: A Doomguide of Kelemvor and a gravedigger's daughter, who learned death as a craft before she learned it as a faith. She has spent her life being *correct* and is no longer sure correct and good are the same word. Joins from the start. Gains approval for honoring the dead, mercy, and admitting doubt.

codex.title: Roen Alleywind
codex.body: An orphan of the Outer City the Harpers chose at fourteen â charm, lockpicks, and an exit built into every sentence. His whole life trained him that attachment is a liability; you are the bet he didn't mean to make. Gains approval for loyalty-under-fire and the truth he hates telling.

codex.title: Varra
codex.body: A tiefling warlock sold to a Hell at six years old by a broker with a kind voice and too many teeth. She tests everyone cruelly, certain she's unlovable because she's *priced.* Won not by grand gestures but by staying through the tests. Gains approval for proving her worth isn't her cost.

codex.title: Naeve of the Seventh Enclave
codex.body: A Netherese arcanist who helped derive the theorem Karsus weaponized â her clean mathematics, his unclean ambition, a sky that fell. She needs to learn some mistakes are *carried,* not erased. Gains approval for intellectual honesty, restraint, and truth over comfort.

codex.title: Ilfaeril
codex.body: A lord of the elven high courts who raised his hand, one terrible afternoon of the Crown Wars, to vote a people's souls *unmade* â and has spent ten thousand years as a half-ghost trying to atone. He must learn atonement is not self-erasure. Gains approval for mercy, oaths kept, and accepting forgiveness.

codex.title: Maerin
codex.body: The girl you pulled out of the Wall in the Breach â luminous, half-dissolved, and entirely her own. She is the proof that the unclaimed can be reclaimed, and the living reminder of what it cost. She is not your reason. She insists on that.

codex.title: Garrow â after the tribunal
codex.body: She put Kelemvor's own canon on trial and lived with the verdict. Whether she kept the grey, took the doctrine back, or walked out from under thirty years, the woman who came out the far side no longer mistakes 'correct' for 'good' â and tends the dead by her own conscience, not the church's.

codex.title: Roen â after the safehouse
codex.body: The Wrenna affair forced the question every con avoids: which lie do you keep, and who pays for it? However he chose, the man stopped building exits into his sentences. The forger finally let one thing be the original â and it terrifies him more than any heist.

codex.title: Varra â after the reckoning
codex.body: Twenty years of a clock in her chest, and she met the collector on her own terms at last â leash turned, debt carried, or contract burned. Whatever the price now reads, she sets it herself. The first transaction of her life with no fine print.

codex.title: Naeve â after the shard
codex.body: She stopped trying to redo the proof of her own life. Whether she rekindled the last Weave, let the sky finish falling, or froze it mid-fall, the arithmetic of grief finally balanced â not solved, carried. A thousand years late, she let Netheril be past tense.

codex.title: Ilfaeril â after the forgiveness
codex.body: Maerith's mercy reached him across ten thousand years â as a door, a sword, or a weight he could not set down. However he answered it, the rigid ancient thing in him moved for the first time in an age. Penitent still, perhaps; but no longer entirely alone in it.

codex.title: Garrow â the one she'll keep
codex.body: The woman who buried everyone and believed in keeping no one learned, late and against all her training, to keep one person. Your name is on the *other* list now â the one of people she refuses to lay down. She has never once meant a promise about the dead. She means this one.

codex.title: Roen â the con he won't run
codex.body: He builds exits into every sentence â except, lately, the ones he says to you. The forger who buried his own first name decided to let one thing be the original. Staying is the bravest con he's ever run, and the only one he refuses to fold.

codex.title: Naeve â the revised axiom
codex.body: She modelled grief as a decay function for a thousand years; you are the variable that broke the model and the first she has no wish to solve. She amended her oldest premise by hand: *you are meaning. Specific. Demonstrable. Checked.* She would unmake a second world before admitting it aloud.

codex.title: Varra â off the books
codex.body: A soul that was a line item since she was six chose you freely â the first transaction of her life with no fine print at all. Whatever she's worth now, she sets it, off the books, un-priceable. She still won't say the soft thing out loud. She just stopped sitting nearest the exit when you're in the room.

codex.title: The Unbound Maw
codex.body: The thing at the bottom of the Cinderhaunt â the first real horror the Returned ever puts down. Not a demon, not undead exactly: a *hunger* that the harvest fed and forgot to leash, grown fat on the souls that didn't make it out with you. It is the saga's whole thesis in miniature, met on the very first night: a cruelty no one built on purpose, that just *accreted* in a place where the dead were treated as fuel. You will meet its big brother at the end of the world. It is called the Unmade.

codex.title: The Cinderhaunt Restless
codex.body: The harvest beneath Baldur's Gate did not take only you. The Cinderhaunt's dead rose with you â shambling things, half-rotted, more confused than cruel, clawing toward the crack you tore on your way back. They are not your enemies, exactly. They are your *fellow-travellers,* the ones who didn't make it all the way out. You put them down with a tenderness that surprises you. There but for a stubborn soul go you.

codex.title: Netherese Arcanists
codex.body: The enclave's defenders, certain to the last heartbeat of a falling city that their borrowed magic could not also fall. They wield shattering glass-bursts and lances of stolen lightning â AoE death that punishes a clustered party. They are not evil. They are a thousand years of hubris given a wand and ten seconds to live.

codex.title: The Echoes
codex.body: Not clones â *arguments.* In the Mirror, the loop throws back twisted versions of your own party: the choices you didn't make, the cruelties you nearly chose, your companions as they'd be if you had failed them. Fighting them is the strangest combat in the saga, because every hit lands on a road not taken. They do not die so much as get *answered* â and the Last Returned that leads them kneels, rather than falling, once its Echoes are spent. It has fought this fight before. It knows how it ends. It is only checking whether, this time, you do too.

codex.title: Elven Blade-Singers
codex.body: Warrior-mages of the Crown Wars, beautiful and lethal and utterly sure their cause is just â radiant smites and moonbows in service of a court that is, that very hour, voting a people into nothing. The tragedy of the Crown Wars is not that monsters fought it. It is that *these* did, and called it law.

codex.title: Unmade Aberrations
codex.body: In the Spellplague's blue fire, where cause forgets to come before effect, the Unmade's grief takes half-shapes â limbs that end in question marks, faces that haven't decided whose they are. They are not creatures so much as *unfinished sentences,* lashing at anything still confident enough to be a whole thing. Killing them feels less like victory than like finishing a thought for someone who can't.

codex.title: Wall-Wraiths
codex.body: Not quite ghosts â the half-dissolved residue of souls the Wall has been eating for centuries, snagged on the edge of forgetting. They drift the Fugue's grey marches, reaching for any warmth that still has a name. They are not malicious. They are *hungry for inclusion* â which, the longer you look, is somehow worse.

codex.title: The Avatar-Touched
codex.body: During the Time of Troubles the gods walked Toril as flesh, and what they touched took on a sliver of the divine â and the divine madness with it. Dangerous, and pitiable.

codex.title: The Mythallar Colossus
codex.body: The last guardian of a flying city's magic-engine, woven of gold and bound arcana â still executing its final order to defend the core, ten heartbeats from the ground. It did not know the city was already dead. You taught it. It was, in the end, only following a command no one was left to rescind.

codex.title: The First Unmade
codex.body: The soul the elven high court voted, first of all, to erase â risen from the half-place between death and nothing, carrying ten thousand years of a grief that was never permitted a grave. It is not evil. It is the first wound of the Wall, given just enough shape to ask *why.* Ilfaeril could not answer it. He could only help you lay it down.

codex.title: The Avatar of Bone
codex.body: During the Time of Troubles the gods walked Toril as flesh â and could die as flesh. At the forge where Myrkul's skull was beaten into the Crown of Horns, a shard of that dying divinity rose to guard the work: a thing of bone and god-madness, neither fully avatar nor fully dead. You proved it could be the latter.

codex.title: The Herald of the Unmade
codex.body: Not a creature so much as a *direction* given teeth â the Unmade's appetite, wearing enough shape to fight in the place where causality runs thin. It does not hate you. It simply wants the erasing finished, and you are the one soul that walked back out of the Wall to argue. You met it in the blue fire and held. It will remember that holding.

codex.title: Blue Fire (the Spellplague)
codex.body: Where the Weave tore, causality runs like wet ink. Blue fire lashes at random, and two things that should be in different places simply swap. Not a creature â a place that bites.

codex.title: Karsus's Folly (Netheril)
codex.body: â339 DR: the archwizard Karsus cast a spell to become a god, and for one heartbeat he succeeded â then magic died, and the flying cities fell out of the sky. You walked one down.

codex.title: The Crown Wars
codex.body: The elven civil wars that birthed the drow â and, in this saga, the first time a court voted to damn a soul to nothing. The Wall was not handed down. It was decided, by people.

codex.title: The Vault of Tens
codex.body: The Lady's riddle room, after the fashion of older, crueler vaults. Place the tokens, speak the answers â but know that with her, wit beats correctness. She would rather be amused than agreed with.

codex.title: The Doom of Kelemvor
codex.body: Kelemvor is the current god of the dead â successor to Myrkul, judge of every soul that comes to the City of Judgement. His Doom is supposed to be fair: none shall pass unjudged. But the Wall is what the Doom does to the unclaimed â discard, not judge â and that gap between the doctrine and the deed is the wound the whole saga presses on.

codex.title: The Wall Was a Decision
codex.body: The deepest, most dangerous idea in the saga: the Wall of the Faithless was not handed down by the gods. It was *voted* â in elven high courts during the Crown Wars, a verdict that hardened over millennia into cosmic law. What people decided, people can un-decide. That is either hope or apocalypse, depending entirely on who is holding the pen.

codex.title: The Time of Troubles
codex.body: 1358 DR: Ao cast the gods down to walk Toril as mortals, and mortals could kill them. Myrkul died screaming over Waterdeep â and his skull was forged into the Crown of Horns. Gods, it turns out, are no more permanent than the Wall they keep. Everything can be buried. Even the burier.

codex.title: The Spellplague
codex.body: 1385 DR: Mystra murdered, the Weave torn, and for a generation magic ran like wet ink â blue fire, floating stone, cause limping after effect. It is where reality is thinnest, and so where the Unmade comes closest to finishing its erasing. The place the rules forgot to hold.

codex.title: The Breach
codex.body: It is possible to reach into the Wall and pull one soul back out. It is not possible to do so without a price, and the price is not gold. The Wall takes someone you love in trade, and it chooses by who you loved most. Authored, not arbitrary.

codex.title: The Lower City
codex.body: The Gate below the walls, where the Flaming Fist doesn't bother to patrol and the temples send their bills before their blessings. Home to the godless, the priced-out, and the grieving â the exact people the Wall of the Faithless is built to forget. Whatever you are to the gods, down here you are simply whoever the streets decide you are. They keep a ledger.

codex.title: The Almshouse of the Unclaimed
codex.body: A candlelit refuge run by Mother Cass, where the godless poor keep each other warm against a cosmos that would erase them. Its heart is the Wall of Names: a plastered wall crowded with the unclaimed dead, written and rewritten by the living who refuse to let them dissolve. It is the whole argument of your saga, made by people who never heard your name.

codex.title: The Faithless Choir
codex.body: Street-evangelists of the Unmade who preach to the Gate's grieving poor that the Wall can fall â and hold the Returned up as living proof. Grief is their recruiter; despair, their scripture. Suppress them and they harden; speak for them and they remember. The Unmade's first roots in the present, growing where the temples left the soil bare.

codex.title: The Hollow Cantor
codex.body: When the Choir's preaching curdled into a militant cell, it found a leader in the undercroft: a hollowed thing that had stopped grieving and started recruiting martyrs. You broke it before it could make one. The Lower City slept easier, and never quite knew why.

codex.title: The Grave-Tithe
codex.body: The quiet racket by which the poor are charged for a consecrated grave â pay, or your dead wait Faithless in the pauper's pit, halfway to the Wall already. The Doom never charged for rest; the church just read the part of the canon that paid. A small mirror of the whole cosmic cruelty: a price put on whether the dead are allowed to be remembered.

codex.title: The Shrunken Quarter
codex.body: A thin market that has seen better centuries, where a god's statue was once pried from its niche and sold in a lean year â and the poor leave offerings to the empty alcove anyway. Faith in the Gate is not about whether anyone is listening. It is about refusing to stop being kind in case.

codex.title: The Chionthar Docks
codex.body: The working waterfront where the river meets the Gate. The dockfolk knot a cord for every drowned pauper the church wouldn't bury â the Chionthar keeps them for free, they say, which is more than the Doom ever did. Even the river, it turns out, remembers the dead better than the law does.

codex.title: High Doomguide Vayle
codex.body: The church's iron conscience â the senior Doomguide who will, at the Court, defend the Wall to the last with the most dangerous argument there is: *she is not wrong about the alternatives.* Tear the Wall down carelessly and death itself unbinds; the Unmade is real and patient and worse than the cruelty that contains it. Vayle is not a villain. She is the part of you that knows the cost of mercy, given a voice and a sword. The hardest ending in the saga is the one where you stand with her, understand her completely, and keep the oldest atrocity in the world because she made the case and you could not break it.

codex.title: Kelemvor, Lord of the Dead
codex.body: The current god of death â once a mortal man, Kelemvor Lyonsbane, who took the office after the Time of Troubles. He inherited the Wall of the Faithless; he did not build it. By all accounts he is a *kind* god, as gods of death go â he tried, early, to make judgement gentler, and the cosmos itself (and Jergal's old contracts) pushed back until he hardened into the role. That is the quiet horror Garrow serves and Aldric rages against: not a cruel god, but a kind one who could not make the machine kind, and stopped trying. The question the saga puts to him is the one he stopped asking: *why keep a cruelty you inherited, simply because you inherited it?*

codex.title: The Faithless and the False
codex.body: Two different sins, the doctrine insists. The *False* betrayed their god â broke an oath, turned coat â and are punished in torment. The *Faithless* simply never knelt to anyone, and are not punished at all: they are *erased,* mortared into the Wall to dissolve. The cosmos reserves its cruelest fate not for the wicked but for the unaffiliated. That distinction â that you must belong to *someone* to be allowed to keep existing â is the rotten beam the whole saga leans on, and means to pull out.

codex.title: The Fugue Plane
codex.body: The grey waystation where every mortal soul arrives at death, to wait under the Crystal Spire until its god comes to claim it. The faithful are gathered. The False and the Faithless are not â and the longer they wait unclaimed, the closer the slow walk to the Wall. You have stood here as a *living* thing, which is its own quiet blasphemy, and walked back out, which is a louder one.

codex.title: The City of Judgement
codex.body: Kelemvor's seat, where the dead are weighed and sorted â paradise, punishment, or the Wall. It was meant to be just. Justice, run long enough by people who stopped reading their own canon, hardened into procedure; and procedure, asked what to do with a soul no god will sign for, found the cheapest answer and called it law. The whole saga is the argument with that answer.

codex.title: The Crack in Time
codex.body: The dead do not come back; you did, and the wrongness of that left a fault line. When you Skip into the fallen ages, you are not time-travelling so much as *falling through your own broken seam* into the moments the Wall was made â Netheril, the Crown Wars, the forge, the blue fire. You cannot change what happened. But you were never meant to *witness* it either, and witness, the Lady would say, is a kind of verdict all its own. Every age you walk, you are quietly entering it into a record someone is keeping.

codex.title: Aldric's Daughter
codex.body: Her name was Elaine. She believed in nothing â not out of spite, just an honest, stubborn unbelief her father, a Doomguide of Kelemvor, could never argue her out of and loved her too much to try. When she died, the Doom her father had served his whole life sorted her with the Faithless and mortared her into the Wall to dissolve. Aldric watched. Everything he has become since â the hunt for the Crown, the willingness to spend the living â is one man's refusal to accept that the universe was *allowed* to do that to his daughter for the crime of being honest. The horror is that he is not wrong.

codex.title: Jergal, the Scribe of the Dead
codex.body: Before Myrkul, before Kelemvor, the dead were kept by Jergal â the first god of the end, who grew so *bored* of eternity's bookkeeping that he handed the whole office away and took a clerk's stool instead. He is still there, filing, faintly amused, ten thousand years into a retirement no one noticed. If any power in the multiverse would find the rewriting of the Law a delightful way to pass an age, it is the one who has been waiting, patiently, for someone interesting to ask.

codex.title: The Ash Pact
codex.body: The infernal compact that owns Varra's soul â a Hell's standing offer to the Gate's desperate poor: power now, collection later, the fine print written in a language no six-year-old could read and no court will overturn. It is not, the devils insist, *cruelty.* It is a *contract,* freely entered, terms disclosed. The saga keeps noticing how often the worst things wear that exact defense â the Wall, the tithe, the Pact â all of them just paperwork, all of them just the rules, all of them a choice someone made and called inevitable so they'd never have to make it again.

codex.title: The Weave
codex.body: Magic in the Realms is not raw â it is *mediated,* drawn through the Weave, the goddess Mystra's living lattice laid over reality so that mortals can touch power without being unmade by it. It is a mercy and a leash both. Twice in the saga you watch it tear: once when Karsus murders its first keeper and Netheril falls, and again when Mystra is murdered a second time and the **Spellplague** boils causality loose. The pattern the Returned cannot stop noticing: every catastrophe in Realms history is some proud hand reaching past the mediator â past the leash, past the law â to grab the power directly. The Wall is only the same reach aimed at the dead.

codex.title: The Mythallar
codex.body: The engine of Netheril's glory: a great captured node of raw magic that let any dabbler wield an archmage's power, and held the flying cities aloft. It made an empire of wonders in a few generations â and made that empire brittle, dependent, and blind, because when a god-mad mortal named Karsus reached up to *steal divinity itself,* the Weave faltered and every mythallar in the sky died at once. The cities fell in a single afternoon. Naeve watched hers come down. The lesson the Realms refused to learn from it is the one the whole saga keeps re-teaching: borrowed power, uncounted, always comes due â and never to the one who borrowed it.

codex.title: The Seventh Enclave
codex.body: One of the floating cities of Netheril, kept aloft by a mythallar's borrowed magic â gardens and spires and a thousand years of arcane certainty, hung in the sky like an argument no one expected to lose. Naeve was its finest mind. She watched it fall when Karsus reached for godhood and the Weave let go. Every word in her language is now a tombstone for it.

codex.title: The Court of the First Damnation
codex.body: An elven high court of the Crown Wars, where â in a clean, well-lit room, by orderly vote â the multiverse first decided that a soul could be *unmade* rather than merely judged. They called it mercy: better nothing than torment. Ilfaeril raised his hand from the third bench. He has spent ten thousand years learning the word they should have used instead. *Cowardice.*

codex.title: What Love Is, in This Story
codex.body: The Breach taught you the saga's cruelest grammar: here, love is never a reward. It is a *stake.* To let someone matter is to hand the cosmos a lever it will, sooner or later, pull. The brave thing is not refusing to love â it is loving anyway, with both eyes open, knowing the bill it runs up. The whole ending comes to rest, finally, on whichever face you were reckless enough to choose.

codex.title: Why the Dead Stay Gone
codex.body: You are the Returned because, somewhere, the loop keeps failing the same way: a soul too stubborn or too loved to dissolve, thrown back through the crack to try again. The dead stay gone because staying gone is a *choice* almost no one is given and fewer still can make. The Lady reads to find out if, this time, you'll be the one who can â and whether she wants you to be.

codex.title: The Loop Remembers
codex.body: You have walked this saga to its end before â and begun again. The world does not know it; the Lower City mud is the same mud, the harvest the same harvest. But something in the white space between the lines kept the count. The Lady always said she was only watching how it ends. She never said you'd only get to find out once. Whatever you chose last time, the wheel turned, and here you are at the start of it, carrying nothing but the shape of what you learned.
